narrative: As a white male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, predicting their likelihood of passing on the first try. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 3.00, LSAT score of 35, Year 1 school decile of 6th (slightly above average), Year 3 decile of 9th, and plans to attend full-time—and calculated a 64% probability that you would fail the bar exam. This high risk of failure is why your application was rejected; law schools use this prediction to prioritize admits likely to succeed and pass the bar promptly. The three most influential factors in pushing your prediction toward failure were your undergraduate GPA, your Year 1 school decile ranking, and your Year 3 school decile ranking. Your undergraduate GPA, which sits below the typical 3.21 average, had the strongest pull toward predicting bar failure, as lower GPAs in undergrad often signal challenges in handling rigorous legal coursework and exam prep. Meanwhile, your Year 1 decile of 6 (a bit better than the average 5.71) nudged the prediction somewhat toward failure compared to top performers, though not as dramatically. Interestingly, your Year 3 decile of 9—well above the average 5.53—worked strongly in your favor, pulling the odds toward bar passage by showing later academic improvement. Your LSAT of 35 (below the 36.7 average) and full-time status (common among 93% of students) played supporting roles but weren't the main drivers. Compared to typical students who pass the bar, your profile shows solid late-stage grades but earlier inconsistencies that raised red flags for the model. To strengthen future applications, focus on boosting your GPA through post-baccalaureate courses or a master's program, retake the LSAT for a higher score, and gather strong letters highlighting your upward trajectory since Year 3. Consider part-time programs if that fits your situation better, or target schools with holistic reviews that weigh your personal growth as a white male from an upper-middle background. With targeted improvements, you can shift that 64% failure risk downward and open new doors. Keep pushing—you've already shown you can climb academic ranks.
